coredata.dc:title: On the effect of substrate thermal conductivity and nano-region on subcooled nucleate boiling in zero-gravity conditions
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer

Urbano, Annafederica
coredata.dc:description: Prediction of heat transfer in configurations of nucleate boiling is a topic that still require new studies for a better understanding of the overall process, which involves multiscale interactions between different physical phenomena. In particular, for space applications, that imply microgravity conditions, experimental data are limited, and numerical simulations offer an efficient alternative to investigate nucleate boiling in such conditions. In the present work, the impact of the nano-region heat flux at the contact line on the equivalent bubble radius is thoroughly studied by means of numerical simulations. A parametric study is carried out by varying several physical variables. From these numerical data, the strong impact of the nano-region heat flux on the overall bubble growth is highlighted, as well as the strong coupling between the nano-region and the thermal conductivity ratio between the liquid and the solid substrate. Finally, a correlation on the bubble size at equilibrium state is drawn, and the heat transfer coefficient is characterized. This enables to show the heat transfer enhancement related to the nano-region.

head.ce:title: On the effect of substrate thermal conductivity and nano-region on subcooled nucleate boiling in zero-gravity conditions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Prediction of heat transfer in configurations of nucleate boiling is a topic that still require new studies for a better understanding of the overall process, which involves multiscale interactions between different physical phenomena. In particular, for space applications, that imply microgravity conditions, experimental data are limited, and numerical simulations offer an efficient alternative to investigate nucleate boiling in such conditions. In the present work, the impact of the nano-region heat flux at the contact line on the equivalent bubble radius is thoroughly studied by means of numerical simulations. A parametric study is carried out by varying several physical variables. From these numerical data, the strong impact of the nano-region heat flux on the overall bubble growth is highlighted, as well as the strong coupling between the nano-region and the thermal conductivity ratio between the liquid and the solid substrate. Finally, a correlation on the bubble size at equilibrium state is drawn, and the heat transfer coefficient is characterized. This enables to show the heat transfer enhancement related to the nano-region.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heat transfer at a solid frontier interacting with a liquid phase and a vapor phase is a topic of great interest in many industrial applications, as spray cooling devices, internal combustion engines or heat exchangers in nuclear industry. All at once experimental, numerical and theoretical developments are still required to improve characterization of such flows and to provide physical insights on the prominent phenomena impacting the whole process. Indeed, as many complex physical effects strongly interact, detailed studies enable to clearly identify the key-mechanisms on which are based liquid-vapor wall heat transfer. In particular, one of the main challenge that must be faced when investigating such phenomena is related to the multi-scale features involved whether nucleate boiling, impacting droplets or film boiling are considered. Indeed, impacting droplets can levitate above a superheated wall due to the formation of a vapor layer entrapped between the droplet and the heated wall. The thickness of this vapor layer is one or two orders of magnitude smaller than the droplet characteristic size [1–3]. This makes numerical simulations of Leidenfrost droplets challenging to perform, due to the large range of relevant spatial scales that must be resolved. However, new physical insights can be expected from fully resolved simulations, and significant endeavors are made in this direction, as shown in the following recent works [4–6].

Relatedly, film boiling presents many common features with Leidenfrost droplets, whereas it occurs in cooling systems operating with a liquid flow. It consists in a vapor layer insulating a superheated solid object immersed in a liquid. Fully resolved numerical simulations of film boiling are also a valuable tool to characterize the occurrence of film boiling as in [7,8], as well as heat transfer and vapor flow inside the vapor film, as illustrated in [9,10], for example. However, such simulations are hardly practicable in industrial configurations, currently, due to the huge range of spatial scales involved in the whole process, from some tens of microns for the vapor layer thickness, until several meters for the length of the solid objects along which a vapor film is formed.

If we consider now numerical simulations of nucleate boiling which are the focus of the present paper, analogous multi-scale challenges must be faced. Indeed, as experimentally highlighted in [11–16] and numerically confirmed in [17,18], two main nucleate boiling regimes can be observed, that is the contact line regime and the microlayer regime. In the first regime, bubbles are growing slowly in such a way that they are maintaining a quasi-steady shape, as observed in several experiments [19–22]. This can be related to an equilibrium static force balance, as reported in [23]. In the second regime a long and thin liquid layer, which thickness is typically between 1 to
10
μ
m and length millimetered-size, is formed between the bubble and the heated wall due to the difference between the bubble expansion velocity and the contact line velocity, as detailed in the following Refs. [12,17,24]. Although the physical mechanisms underlying the formation of this thin liquid film differ completely of the thin vapor layer in the Leidenfrost or film boiling regimes, both share some common features in terms of numerical resolution since fully-resolved simulations require huge computational resources due to the large range of spatial scales involved.

On the other hand, one might imagine that achieving numerical simulations in the contact line regime is an easier task since in this regime, bubbles exhibit more simpler shapes and no very thin layer has to be described. However, numerical modeling of the contact line regime has its own challenges, among which one should mention the description of a microregion or nano-region in the vicinity of the contact line. Whereas the denomination microregion is widespread in the literature, we think that the denomination nano-region is a better choice, to avoid confusion with the microlayer which is much longer and thicker. Indeed, the nano-region is a submicrometric region, with a typical dimension smaller than 100 nm, in the vicinity of the contact line where intense heat transfer and phase change occur resulting in a modification of the interface shape at very low scale. This is why the denomination nano-region will be used in the rest of this paper. Experimental evidences of the existence of this nano-region are difficult to provide due to its tiny size. This makes out of reach both a direct measurement of the heat flux associated to this region, and also a full resolution of a bubble growth coupled to a nano-region at the contact line, unlike microlayer which are much longer and thicker. However, a typical feature that suggests the existence of a nano-region is the variation of a measured contact angle with the substrate temperature, such as reported in [25]. Indeed, most existing nanoregion models, as the ones presented in [26–28], predict a variation of the interface slope inside the nanoregion which depends on the local superheat. This results in an apparent contact angle, as a correction of the microscopic contact angle at the contact line. Besides this correction of the contact angle, such models also predict a nano-region heat flux which depends on the local superheat. As previously stated, a complete resolution of the bubble growth in the contact line regime is not possible until the scale of the nano-region, therefore some coupling strategies between a Direct Numerical Simulation solver (DNS-solver) and a nano-region model have to be considered. Such implementations have been proposed in the following Refs. [19,29–32]. In particular, in [31], the strong impact of the nano-region heat flux on the growth of a bubble in microgravity conditions is highlighted drawing on comparisons with the RUBI experiment [33] carried out in the International Space Station. Such trends have already been reported in [34] which suggests a contribution of the nano-region around
∼ 50 %
of the total vaporization mass flow rate. It is worth mentioning that in partially subcooled conditions, for which stabilized bubbles can be observed due to the equilibrium between boiling and condensation, the relative contribution of the nano-region to the total vaporization mass flow rate increases. This can be explained simply, by remarking that vaporization is distributed on a smaller part of the bubble surface than for saturated or superheated conditions. In [35], numerical investigations in such subcooled and microgravity conditions were performed. The numerical simulations show the stabilization of bubble due to the subtle equilibrium between vaporization and condensation along the bubble interface. However, this former study does not consider nano-region effects, since numerical simulations have been restricted to low thermal conductivity ratios between the solid substrate and the liquid. Indeed, in [31], it was shown that the impact of the nano-region is significant for high thermal conductivity ratios, but can be neglected if low thermal conductivity ratios are considered. This can be explained by remarking that the nano-region occurrence results from a breakage of the Local Thermodynamics Equilibrium at the contact line, which is favored by strong differences between thermal conductivities of solid substrate and of the liquid. Significant effects of the wall thermophysical properties have also been reported in several anterior works [36–38].

It is worth noting that several numerical studies have been satisfactorily conducted on nucleate boiling in contact line regime without using a nano-region model. For instance, in [23], nucleate boiling of water in normal gravity conditions was simulated and a very good agreement with experimental data in terms of detachment diameter was found. High microscopic contact angles were considered for water, which is not a wetting fluid. Consequently, as detailed in [31], the introduction of a nano-region model in such simulations mainly impacts the detachment frequency of the bubble but has only a low impact on the bubble detachment diameter. Another configuration which was satisfactorily simulated verifying an analytical model was sub-cooled boiling in micro-gravity conditions with a very low conductivity ratio between the solid material and the liquid, for which the nano-region is not expected to act, as it was demonstrated in [31]. To summarize, the nano-region is expected to play a major role on nucleate boiling in sub cooled conditions in micro-gravity, in particular for wetting fluids and high conductivity ratios between the solid and the liquid. These are precisely the conditions that are of interest for several applications, among which space propellant tanks and will be the focus of the present study.

In the present study, we propose an extension of the previous work presented in [35], where DNS where carried out without using a nano-region model and only for low thermal conductivity of the wall. Using the numerical framework developed in [31], accounting for the nano-region at the contact line, in the present work numerical investigations in configurations with a much higher and realistic thermal conductivity ratio between the solid substrate and the liquid phase are performed. In particular, numerical simulations in subcooled conditions confirm the strong impact of the nano-region on the stabilized bubble radius and so on the wall heat flux for a large thermal conductivity ratio. In a second part, a semi-empirical model, based on numerical data and taking into account the contribution of the nano-region, is developed for the bubble radius equilibrium and the related Nusselt number.

In this section, the conservation equations and the associated jump conditions at the liquid vapor interface for two-phase flows modeling are reminded. The coupling with a nano-region model at the contact line, already presented in [31], is also quickly described.

Incompressible Navier–Stokes equations are considered for the description of nucleate boiling phenomena of interest for which small temperature variations and small Mach number flows are involved. In both liquid phase and vapor phase, the mass conservation equation is written,

The simulations are performed using an incompressible solver for boiling simulations, implemented in our in-house code DIVA . Interface motion is solved with a Level-Set Method (LSM) [40,41] by transporting a distance level function ϕ , which zero-level (
ϕ = 0
) corresponds to the interface position. A redistance algorithm is used at each iteration to maintain the distance properties of ϕ . A Ghost-Fluid Method (GFM) [42–45], suitable for boiling simulations [46–48], enables to impose jump conditions across the interface and to maintain sharpness of the liquid-vapor interface and accurate computations of the gradients of discontinuous variables across the interface. The temperature field is solved in the liquid and vapor phases, imposing a Dirichlet boundary condition at the interface
T
l , Γ
=
T
v , Γ
=
T
s a t
, and then extrapolated in the other phase using a quadratic extension, as proposed by Aslam in [49], to populate the ghost fields. The interest of such high order extrapolation has been demonstrated in [48] in the framework of boiling simulations. The phase change mass flow rate
m
̇
is computed using the jump condition Eq. (10) and then used to extend the velocity field as proposed in [50]. The linear system for the pressure obtained from the pressure projection method is solved using a Black Box Multi-Grid algorithm [51]. Finally, convective terms are discretized with WENO-Z schemes [52], and diffusion terms with second order centered Finite Volume schemes.

This solver has been largely validated for the simulation of phase change in incompressible fluids, with the simulations of various numerical benchmarks [48], and validation against experimental data including boiling in normal gravity conditions [23], and droplets impacting on a hot wall in Leidenfrost regime [4]. It has been successfully used to investigate boiling phenomena in both contact line and micro-layer regime [17,35], in normal and microgravity conditions. Some recent extensions of this solver to compressible two-phase flows can also be found in the following Refs. [53,54].

The nano-region has a size of the order of tens of nanometers, and its greater influence is felt in the first nanometers after the contact line. As a direct resolution of physical phenomena at such low scales raises many fundamental and practical issues, a method to integrate a nano-region model at the sub-grid level in the DNS solver has been recently presented in [31] and is briefly summarized here. The set of ordinary differential equations describing the nano-region that are implemented in the solver have been introduced by Stephan & Busse in [26] for perfectly wetting fluids only and then extended by Mathieu in [27] to describe partially wetting fluids. The nano-region model of Mathieu [27] is used in the present work :

for which a smaller impact of the nano-region is expected. For both values, we observe that
θ
a p p
is greater than
θ
m i c
and that there is a positive heat flux associated with the nanoregion. As expected, the higher f , the higher is the heat flux and the variation of the angle.

Note that in the present study, no hysteresis of the microscopic contact angle
θ
m i c
is considered. However, it is known that this could be induced by surface roughness or variable wettability and an hysteresis model could be added in future works.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coupling of the DNS solver and the nano-region model
ce:section.ce:para:
The nano-region model introduced in Section 2.3 is used to generate an integrated sub-grid model to account for the nano-region effects at the contact line in the direct numerical simulation of nucleate boiling. A coupling strategy between both solvers has been presented in Torres et al. [31] where the interested reader can find all the numerical details associated with the implementation, as well as a validation against experimental data from the RUBI experiment [33]. In particular, the previous study showed that the nano-region provides an important contribution to the total bubble vaporization, and must be taken into account. It is important to underline that the nano-region integrated model has to be used with a solver for conjugate heat transfer between the solid substrate and the fluid. A fundamental point is to account for the balance between the heat flux coming from the solid wall and the nano-region heat flux to find the equilibrium wall temperature
T
w
of the solid material at the contact line. Note that
T
w
will be different from the temperature of the interface at the contact line,
T
Γ , C L
, due to thermal resistance
R
Γ
. In particular, it has been shown in [31] that the suitable temperature boundary condition in the cell containing the contact line is a Robin Boundary Condition, since neither the temperature nor the heat flux are known inside this cell when starting the temporal iteration. Once the (
T
w
−
T
Γ , C L
)
is known, the corresponding apparent contact angle
θ
a p p
and nano-region phase change mass flow rate
m
̇
n r
can be evaluated and must be included in the numerical simulation. For
θ
a p p
this is done by imposing the corresponding Neumann boundary condition on the Level Set field at the wall, as described in [23,31]. Concerning the nano-region phase change mass flow rate
m
̇
n r
, it must be included in the direct numerical simulation as a subgrid model when solving the fluid domain. However, it has been shown that including it roughly in a single cell at the contact line led to numerical instabilities and eventually to a lack of energy conservation. In [31], the authors propose to spread
m
̇
n r
in a few cells along the interface from the contact line to overcome these stability issues. A Gaussian function is used to spread the
m
̇
n r
and a sensitivity study has shown that a suitable number of spreading cells lies around five which therefore will be used in the present study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational domain, reference case and parameters
Subcooled nucleate boiling in zero-gravity conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
The present study is focused on subcooled nucleate boiling in microgravity conditions, a configuration that we have previously investigated in Urbano et al. [35]. We remind here briefly the main results obtained in this former study. The schematic is shown in Fig. 3. It consists in a bubble in microgravity conditions which is immersed in a stratified thermal field. In Fig. 3,
T
s a t
is the saturation temperature, the top wall is at
T
s u b
<
T
s a t
and the bottom wall is at
T
w , e q
>
T
s a t
. As a consequence, vaporization occurs in the bottom part of the bubble and condensation on the top part and, in zero-gravity conditions, the bubble will reach an equilibrium diameter when boiling compensates condensation. The compensation between evaporation and condensation leading to an equilibrium status when the bubble diameter is constant in time has been described with an analytical model based on first principles in Urbano et al. [35]. A parametric numerical study was then carried and lead to the following correlation for the bubble equivalent diameter at equilibrium:,
(17)

/

with
T

the equilibrium temperature of the wall, which is the temperature that would reach the solid/liquid interface without bubble. The constant C was derived from the numerical results and it is a correction coefficient which multiplies the analytical model. An extensive study was conducted in Urbano et al. [35] to verify the validity of this model. It was shown that by changing the dimensions of the domain L or the temperature superheat and sub-cooling but keeping the same Ja ratio and
θ
a p p
, the same
R
e q , 0
/ L
was obtained. There were two major limitations in the study of Urbano et al. [35]:

ce:list-item.ce:para: No nano-region model was considered. Consequently the parametric study was limited to imposed contact angles greater than 30°. Indeed, it is generally accepted that for such contact angle values, the difference between the microscopic contact angle and the apparent contact angle resulting from the nano-region is weak.

The ratio between the solid substrate and liquid thermal conductivities,
λ
̃
=
λ
w
λ
l
, was limited to small values around one, to maintain the Local Thermodynamic Equilibrium (LTE) at the contact line. In particular, it is shown that space convergence on the local heat flux at the contact line and local thermalization of the solid substrate by the liquid-vapor interface can be achieved with reasonably refined grids in the solid substrate, for
λ
̃
values around
∼ 1
, whereas it cannot be achieved for higher values of
λ
̃
.

ce:section.ce:para:
Both aforementioned limitations have been recently overcome with the coupling between a nanoregion model and a DNS solver mentioned in the previous sections. In particular, it is shown in [31] that, by relaxing the LTE assumption at the contact line, this coupling enables proper space convergence on the local heat flux at the contact line and so on the bubble radius equilibrium, whatever the
λ
̃
value. Moreover, using a nano-region model lower values of the contact angle, below
30 °
, can be investigated since the nanoregion effects are taken into account.

The objective of the present study is to extend the model provided by Eq. (17) to smaller contact angles θ and higher conductivity ratios
λ
̃
, by accounting for nano-region effects, thanks to the subgrid model developed in [31].

It is worth mentioning that at steady state, numerical solutions should only depend on solid thermal conductivity, and not on the solid density and specific heat, since the latter two properties only appear in unsteady terms. Liquid, vapor and solid properties are summarized in Table 1. The considered value for the surface tension between liquid and vapor is

The initial temperature field is shown in Fig. 5, where the initial bubble nucleus is also reported. In all the simulations the nucleus radius is set to

In the initial condition, based on the initial temperature field, the nano-region model provides the nano-region heat flux and the apparent contact angle which are used for the first iteration.

We consider a case with

In order to assess the space convergence of the simulations, computations have been carried out with different mesh resolutions, that is 256 × 256, 512 × 512 and 1024 × 1024 in the fluid domain. It corresponds, respectively, to cell sizes of 3, 6 and

On the other hand, with a nano-region model very similar results are obtained with the different grids showing the numerical convergence. In Table 2 the corresponding contact line superheat

The bubble diameter initially increases and then, as expected, reaches an equilibrium value when condensation on the top of the bubble compensates for evaporation on the bottom (as analyzed in [35]). The temperature field at equilibrium is shown in Fig. 6 in the left part and the associated velocity vectors are shown in the right side of the figure. The velocity vectors exit from the bubble in the upper part where condensation occurs and enter the bubble in the lower part characterized by vaporization. When vaporization and condensation mass flow rate compensate exactly, the bubble reaches a steady equilibrium diameter. This can also be clearly visualized in Fig. 7, where the phase change mass flow rate
m
̇
has been plotted along a curvilinear abscissa starting from the contact line. One can observe that
m
̇
goes from positive values near the contact line to negative values in the upper part of the bubble where condensation occurs. It is worth noting that the temperature field in Fig. 6 is perturbed with respect to the initial condition in Fig. 5 in particular at the contact line where the solid material is cooled down. This involves an enhanced wall heat transfer, and thus an increase of the Nusselt number, that will be quantified thanks to the numerical simulations in a next section.

A parametric study is carried out to build a correlation for the bubble equilibrium diameter and to study the associated Nusselt numbers, considering the presence of the nano-region. As the nano-region model enables to regularize the thermal singularity at the contact line for large
λ
̃
, this allows investigations on configurations with high solid thermal conductivity
λ
w
, for which space convergence issues were reported in the previous work [35]. The following five parameters are varied for the sake of this study:

ce:list-item.ce:para:
The microscopic contact angle
θ
m i c
, to observe the influence of wetting conditions. The microscopic contact angle depends on the nature of the fluid and the surface, and its value is often highly uncertain. For cryogenics or refrigerants, which are wettable or quasi-wettable fluids, we expect small values between 0 and 10 degrees. On the other hand, water should have a contact angle around 50. Consequently, in the present study three values will be consider: 10, 30 and 50 degrees.

The Jakob ratio
Ja
c
/
Ja
v
: high values indicate larger sub-cooled regions, whereas low values are for higher superheat. In this study the sub-cooling is varied up to 10 K and the superheat up to 2 K, leading to Ja ratios varying between 1 and 10. These are values that appear to be reasonable with respect to generic applications featuring sub-cooled boiling.

The superheat
(
T
w , e q
−
T
s a t
)
since for a given Jakob ratio the nano-region depends on the local superheat and therefore this parameter may have an impact on the overall bubble growth even for a constant
Ja
c
/
Ja
e
.

The ratio between solid and liquid thermal conductivities
λ
̃
=
λ
w
λ
l
which is varied between 1.5 and 150. The ratio
λ
̃
= 150
corresponds to the RUBI experiment, that was simulated in [31]. For
λ
̃
= 150
if the liquid is water, the corresponding solid material is a metal (between iron and copper). A ratio
λ
̃
= 100
corresponds to water/platinum. The smallest ratio
λ
̃
= 1 . 5
corresponds to water and a plastic material. This may be less interesting from an application point of view but fundamental for the present parametric study that aims at analyzing the impact
λ
̃
have on the physics of boiling.

The accommodation coefficient f for the influence of the nano-region. For volatile fluids as refrigerants, f is between 0.5 and 1. For water, a large range of f values can be found in the literature (from 0.001 to 1). Therefore, the rationale in this study has been to test two values: one small value,
f = 0 . 125
, and one high value,
f = 1
.

ce:section.ce:para:
The ranges of variation of the five parameters are reported in Table 3. The thermal conductivity ratio varies with
λ
w
whose values are between 1 and 100 W/m/K.

More than 150 simulations have been performed for the parametric analysis, with a mesh size of
6
μ m
, giving a total mesh 512 × (512 + 256) for
L
r
×
(
L
z
+
L
z , w
)
. For the reference case, to reach the equilibrium bubble radius in steady state, simulations are performed until a physical time of
2 . 5
s
. The computational cost of a simulation is highly dependent on the parameters, but the average cost is around ∼ 2000
h
CPU
per simulation, which means that the CPU cost of the entire study is 300 000
h
CPU
. Simulations are performed on supercomputer clusters using 16 or 32 cores in parallel for each simulation.

A first analysis, to highlight the impact of the parameters over the equilibrium diameter, is performed in this section. The simulations for
θ
m i c
= 10 °
are reported in Fig. 9 for two different
Ja
c
/
Ja
v
ratios, that is 5 and 10, and two different
λ
̃
ratios, 1.5 and 150. The ratio of thermal conductivity changes with the lines, while the Jakob number ratio changes with columns. For each of the four conditions, six curves are drawn for 3 different superheats (
T
w , e q
−
T
s a t
= 1 / 1 . 5 / 2
K) identified by different colors, and for two accommodation parameters (
f = 0 . 125 / 1
) identified by different markers. It is worth mentioning that for a given Jakob number ratio, several couples of the superheat
T
w , e q
−
T
s a t
and of the subcooling
T
s a t
−
T
s u b
can be considered. This enables to assess to what extent the final results depend on the specific value of the wall temperature
T
w , e q
, for a given
T
w , e q
−
T
s a t
. These preliminary results are shown for a given contact angle, but similar trends will be reported in the next section for other values of
θ
m i c
investigated in the parametric study. As previously reported in [35], we see in Fig. 9 that the superheat level, for a constant
Ja
c
/
Ja
v
, does not impact the equilibrium diameter of the bubble, even for high values of f . Indeed, a higher superheat slightly increases the growth rate in the transient stage but not the final equilibrium diameter, as observed in Fig. 9. Moreover, increasing
Ja
c
/
Ja
v
, the sub-cooling becomes more predominant compared to the superheat. As a consequence, the equilibrium diameter decreases. This is also visible in Fig. 10 where two snapshots of the temperature field and bubble interface are shown at equilibrium for a lower (a) or higher (b) Jakob ratio, keeping all the other parameters equal.

However, several new information, related to a strong thermal conductivity in the solid, can be learned from these results. Indeed, results presented in the previous study [35] are restricted to low
λ
̃
, and do not account for the nano-region. It is worth mentioning that both assumptions are consistent, since high
λ
̃
values favor high nano-region heat flux, whereas at low
λ
̃
it tends to vanish, as shown in [31]. The present study enables one to go beyond these assumptions by accounting for the nano-region, following the numerical methods described in [31]. In particular, this parametric study enables to investigate the effect of
λ
̃
and the accommodation coefficient f . The first trends observed in Fig. 9, show that the bubble equilibrium diameter increases strongly with
λ
̃
. For example, the bubble equilibrium diameter is doubled between
λ
̃
= 1 . 5
and
λ
̃
= 150
for
Ja
c
/
Ja
v
= 10
. Second, the impact of the accommodation coefficient f is coupled to
λ
̃
. For low values of
λ
̃
(bottom), the influence of f is negligible, since the full and dotted lines lead to the same equilibrium diameter. This is consistent with a vanishing nano-region effect at such low values of
λ
̃
. In contrast, high values of
λ
̃
entail an increasing impact of f , with up to 20% difference in the equilibrium diameter (thus around 70% on the bubble volume) between a low and a high value of f . We believe this is an important observation showing how the effect of a nano-region is strongly coupled to the conjugate heat transfer between the solid and the fluid.

However, from this first analysis it appears that the five parameters under consideration are entangled with each other, and individual conclusions are difficult to draw. Consequently, in what follows, the influence of the different parameters independently is conducted in order to investigate local effects they may have in terms of superheat at the contact line and phase change mass flow rate. Next, a correlation for the bubble equilibrium radius and associated heat transfer behavior will be extracted from these numerical data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evolution of the contact line superheat and nano-region fluxes
ce:section.ce:para:
It is worth reminding that
T
w , e q
is the temperature at the solid–liquid interface in the initial condition, and will remain unperturbed far from the bubble. When a bubble is nucleated, solid–liquid interface temperature is perturbed and we indicate with
T
w
its value at the contact line, and
T
w
−
T
s a t
the superheat at the contact line, with,
T

<

w
is an output of the simulation which can vary in time. In this section, we investigate the impact of
λ
̃
and f on the value of the contact line superheat
T
w
−
T
s a t
and on the associated nano-region phase change mass flow rate.

As stated previously, the local superheat at the Contact Line

K. On the left plot, the mass flux in the vicinity of the contact line is displayed and on the right plot, its value along the whole bubble interface is plotted. The mass flux decreases rapidly with distance and reaches negative values in the top half of the bubble, where condensation occurs. As was previously suggested, one can observe in Fig. 12 that the contribution of the nano-region, which is visible near the contact line (for

Indeed, for higher

On the other hand, one can observe that higher values of

This can be explained considering that, if the absolute value of the superheat is indeed higher, the associated heat flux is lower, since the interfacial resistance is larger for lower f values (see Eq. ce:section.ce:section-title:
Impact of the superheat
T

−

ce:section.ce:para:
The previous section, analysis was performed for one initial imposed superheat,
T
w , e q
−
T
s a t
, and in this section the influence of its variation is considered. In Fig. 11 it appears that for low values of
λ
̃
the contact line superheat

−

s a t
is always close to 0. As a result, the bubble radius equilibrium only depends on the
Ja
c
/
Ja
v
, no matter the
T
w , e q
−
T
s a t
. This is consistent with the analysis of Fig. 9 (top), on which it has been reported that the equilibrium diameter does not depend on the imposed superheat
T
w , e q
−
T
s a t
. To explain this behavior, the mass flux
m
̇
along the interface is displayed in Fig. 15, for the same
Ja
c
/

v
= 5
,

̃
= 15
and

, but for two different superheats
T

−

of 1 and 2 K.

As expected, higher superheats increase the vaporization flux. The integral mass flux
M
̇
is about twice higher if the superheat is doubled, and so is the nano-region mass flux. However, when normalized with the maximum value of the vaporization mass flux
m
̇
m a x
, the curves with the different superheats are superimposed (Fig. 15 bottom). This demonstrates that the relative contribution of the nano-region to the total vaporization mass flux does not depend on the superheat (for a given
Ja
c
/
Ja
e
). Specifically for both superheats, the nano-region contributes for 38% to the total phase change mass flow rate. However, the nano-region contribution remains proportional to the imposed superheat and the associated increase in the phase change mass flow rate is balanced by the condensation at the top of the bubble.

All the above-described trends are observed for all microscopic contact angles
θ
m i c
investigated. The values in terms of local superheat at the contact line do not differ much, slightly increasing with
θ
m i c
. However, it should be reminded that the nano-region model is also dependent on
θ
m i c
and, as a consequence, also plays a role in the global evolution of the bubble. In fact, the resulting heat flux for a given fluid, changing only its wetting properties, can be widely different. For more details on this specific point, we refer to the more complete studies of Mathieu [27], Janeček [58] or Torres [55].

Nucleate boiling in a subcooled liquid under zero gravity conditions has been investigated with a large parametric study. Direct numerical simulations considering conjugate heat transfer have been performed with a validated solver. The main novelty of the numerical setup is the capability of accounting for a nano-region at the contact line, with a subgrid model. The major result of the present study is the analysis of the importance of the ratio of the conductivity of the solid material to the liquid over the bubble equilibrium diameter and associated heat transfer. Indeed, the occurrence of a nano-region at the contact line is related to a Local Thermodynamic Non-Equilibrium condition at the contact line which induces a temperature discontinuity, and thus an increased local heat flux. Large values of
λ
̃
favor this temperature discontinuity at the contact line, and so the nano-region vaporization flux. As a result, the larger the
λ
̃
, the larger the equilibrium diameter reached by the bubble and the enhancement in terms of heat transfer.

Finally, a correlation of the bubble equilibrium diameter depending on the parameters has been drawn. The heat flux, through the Nusselt number has also been investigated. These two results can be used to predict the heat transfer between a bubble and a superheated wall. One of the parameters of the present work is the accommodation coefficient, inherent to the nano-region model and difficult to know accurately especially for water. Therefore, two values of f have been used to evaluate the sensitivity of the numerical results to this parameter.

Many applications for which nucleate boiling in sub-cooled conditions in micro-gravity is relevant (like, for instance, space launchers tanks) make use of a non–condensable gas for pressurization. Therefore, in such conditions the presence of Marangoni convection could play an important role in micro-gravity conditions which it would be interesting to address in future studies. This will imply numerical developments to extend the solver to multi-component evaporation (presence of multiple species in the liquid and absorption desorption coefficients at the interface).
